story:
At night I would stare at the dark green costume cupboard at the foot of my bed and pretend it was a portal like Narnia’s. I even climbed inside sometimes and rapped on the back boards, just in case. I did it most on Thursdays at quarter to four, fifteen minutes before I had to leave for music lesson. I was five, fresh from my first classical concert, where I had turned to my parents and declared that I wanted to play the violin. I imagine they exchanged a proud look—our child prodigy. A few weeks later my teacher suggested gently that I might try piano instead. I agreed, and still the lessons didn’t last.

My belief in the cupboard didn’t come from Narnia alone. Each night Dad told me a story in his low, calm voice: “Once upon a time there was a little boy called Peter who had a magnificent costume cupboard.” Peter would reach in, choose a sailor’s outfit, step out the door, and find himself aboard a ship in the middle of the sea. He had astonishing adventures but always made it back in time for tea.

Mine didn’t teleport me anywhere, but it was still my favorite thing in the world, the anchor for dull school days and uneasy bedtimes. When I lay there imagining it was magical, I also ran safety drills in my head. If there were a fire, what would I save? Always my dog, Mr. Penguino, and my costume cupboard.

From five to eleven I kept the same after-school routine. At two o’clock I’d walk home, stop at the corner shop for a Kinder Bueno, and eat it with chocolate smeared across my grin. Before our street I’d toss the wrapper, ride the lift, wipe my face in the mirror, greet my frantic dog, and answer my parents’ questions with a quick “Good.” Then I’d open the dark green doors onto a riot of color. Who today—fiery flamenco dancer or pristine ballerina? On Thursdays, later in the afternoon, I would lose myself in the cupboard again before leaving for whatever lesson or club came next.

My best costume was the old granny. I put on a long skirt, a tiny straw hat, wrapped myself in as many scarves as I could, and found a stick to stoop over. I shuffled into the kitchen to fuss with tea things and startled my own parents, who didn’t recognize me at first.

I knew every item by heart: the loose beads on the Turkish costume Mum brought back, the worn holes in granny’s scarves. Still, I gave each piece my full attention, hunting for the day’s role. On Monday I might be Marie Antoinette, tucking Mr. Penguino under my arm, practicing a French accent, and sipping imaginary tea with cake. On Friday I became Mary Poppins, feet turned out, umbrella aloft, humming about a spoonful of sugar.

By late afternoon the spell broke. “Come on, sweetie, we’ve got to go! Why aren’t you dressed?” Half an hour later I’d be circling a track in gray leggings or mumbling songs in Spanish class, silent on the outside while my mind slipped back to finish whatever story I’d left mid-scene.

At eleven we moved countries. The cupboard didn’t come. By thirteen I’d let it fade beneath exams and friends. At sixteen people asked what I wanted to be; at eighteen they wanted to know what I’d study. I never knew. I tried business but could only do the homework if I pretended to be a millionaire signing papers. In Biology, my favorite moments were when we put on white lab coats. The feeling returned then—the same lift I’d felt opening those green doors.

I didn’t go to university that year. I moved back instead, to the country where the cupboard still existed. One day my parents asked, “Do you want to look through your cupboard?” They led me to it—chipped, creaking, smaller inside than I remembered. Yet the same old thrill rose up and sharpened into curiosity, and suddenly I was back in that other world.
